title: WPM Typing Speed Counter (live tracking)
shortDescription: Measure your typing speed in real time. Words per minute (WPM), characters per minute (CPM), accuracy, and elapsed time. Color-coded errors.
description: Type the sample text and the counter tracks your speed live. WPM uses the standard '5 characters per word' convention. Errors are highlighted in red as you type. Reset and try again to track improvement.
metaDescription: Free WPM typing speed counter. Real-time WPM, CPM, accuracy %, elapsed time. Color-coded errors. Practice typing speed and track improvement.

sections.heading: How WPM is calculated
paragraphs: WPM (words per minute) uses a standardized 'word' = 5 keystrokes including spaces. So if you type 250 characters in 1 minute, that's 250/5 = 50 WPM. This convention dates back to typewriter testing and is used by virtually all typing tests.

CPM (characters per minute) is the raw character count divided by minutes. Easier to track for languages where word boundaries are unclear (Japanese, Chinese, Korean), and useful for code typing where keystroke count matters more than 'word' count.

sections.heading: Typical WPM benchmarks
paragraphs: Beginner: 0-25 WPM. Hunt-and-peck typing, looking at keyboard.
Average: 35-45 WPM. Most office workers fall here. Two-handed but with occasional glances at the keyboard.
Skilled: 50-70 WPM. Touch typing without looking. Comfortable for most professional work.
Fast: 70-100 WPM. Touch typing fluently. Programmers, writers, and transcriptionists often type in this range.
Elite: 100+ WPM. Competitive typists. Top recorded speeds exceed 200 WPM for short bursts.

sections.heading: Tips to improve
paragraphs: Touch typing first: learn to type without looking at the keyboard. Free resources: typing.com, monkeytype.com, keybr.com. Initial slow-down is normal — speed comes back within 2-3 weeks.
Accuracy before speed: practice slowing down to 90%+ accuracy before pushing speed. Errors waste time on backspace; consistency wins.
Use a comfortable keyboard: mechanical keyboards reduce fatigue for long typing sessions. Layout matters less than fit (Dvorak/Colemak save lateral motion but require relearning).
Practice 15-20 minutes daily. Steady incremental gain beats occasional long sessions. Most plateaus break with a few weeks of consistent practice.

howTo.name: Click in the textarea
howTo.text: Or just start typing — the timer starts at the first keystroke.

howTo.name: Type the sample text
howTo.text: Errors are highlighted in red. Stats update live.

howTo.name: Aim for accuracy
howTo.text: Aiming for 95%+ accuracy is more useful than chasing pure speed.

howTo.name: Reset to try again
howTo.text: Track WPM gains over time. Improvement is usually clear within 1-2 weeks of practice.

faq.q: Why is my WPM lower than expected?
faq.a: Three common reasons: (1) the convention counts characters/5, so symbol-heavy or short-word text gives lower WPM; (2) errors don't reduce typed count but they reduce accuracy; (3) reading the screen and the sample slows you down compared to memorized text.
For office work: 50+ WPM is comfortable. For technical writing/code: 60-80 WPM. Below 40 indicates room for improvement; above 100 is competitive-level.

faq.q: Does this work for Japanese / Chinese?
faq.a: It tracks character-by-character match, so yes. CPM is more meaningful than WPM for non-Latin scripts since 'words' aren't space-separated.

faq.q: Can I customize the sample text?
faq.a: Not in this version — we provide language-appropriate samples. For practice on specific texts, copy the prompt yourself and adapt your typing tests.

faq.q: Why does my accuracy drop on errors?
faq.a: Accuracy = correct characters / total typed. So a single error in 100 chars = 99% accuracy. We compare position-by-position; if you typed 'eht' instead of 'the', that's three errors not one.

faq.q: Should I count errors as half-credit?
faq.a: Different tests do this differently. Some use 'gross WPM' (raw speed) and 'net WPM' (penalized for errors). We show raw WPM/CPM and separate accuracy %.

faq.q: Is it better to type fast or accurately first?
faq.a: Accuracy first. Habits matter — a 50 WPM accurate typist beats a 70 WPM error-prone typist over any sustained task because backspacing eats time.

faq.q: Does the data leave my browser?
faq.a: No. Tracking runs locally; nothing is sent to a server.
